articles.title: A Speed Limit for Computers
articles.summary: A Speed Limit for Computers is a concept that suggests increasing computing power beyond a certain point does not necessarily translate to greater autonomy or social progress. Ivan Illich's 1973 essay argued that energy and equity can grow concurrently only up to a threshold, where increased energy affluence leads to decreased distribution of control over the energy. This idea has been applied to traffic and speed, with restrictions on vehicle speed introduced in the UK to more equitably ration fuel. In computing, restrictions tend to focus on data processing and accountability rather than limiting machine power, but it is unclear how many problems are inherent in increased computing capability.

articles.summary: Embedding information in disorder Disguising information in plain view is a toy problem that has been explored through various methods, including repurposing timestamps and modifying Bluetooth firmware. This technique can be applied to file watermarking and transferability, making it a valuable tool for developers.

Developed by the open-source community and a team of developers who created MapsWithMe / Maps.Me, it's free from ads, tracking, data collection, and other unwanted features. This app is verified by Exodus Privacy Project and doesn't request excessive permissions to spy on users, making it a great option for those seeking privacy and freedom while traveling or exploring the outdoors.

articles.title: Run Windows 2000 on a DEC Alpha with a new es40 fork

The release also includes graphics support without the need for X11 tunneling, making it easier to set up a graphical login system.

articles.title: A 10-year sky survey begins filming a 'cosmic movie,' cyborg cockroaches go for a dive and more science stories
articles.summary: A 10-year sky survey begins filming a 'cosmic movie,' cyborg cockroaches go for a dive and more science stories. The Vera C. Rubin Observatory, which contains the largest digital camera in the world at 3,200 megapixels, has begun its Legacy Survey of Space and Time, capturing images roughly every 40 seconds to create an ultrawide, ultrahigh-definition time-lapse record of the universe. This survey will allow it to record changes and cosmic events over a decade-long period, providing new insights into dark energy and dark matter.
